heuristics.rule: Obtain DSO approval for Reduced Course Load BEFORE dropping any course — never after
heuristics.condition: When an F-1 student faces any situation (medical, academic difficulty, final semester) that may require dropping below full-time enrollment
heuristics.action: Contact your DSO immediately, provide required documentation, and wait for written DSO authorization in SEVIS before submitting any course drop request
heuristics.because: Retroactive RCL approval does not exist in federal regulations — dropping first and asking later results in immediate out-of-status, which requires a USCIS reinstatement application to fix

heuristics.rule: If you have been out of F-1 status, file for USCIS reinstatement within 5 months — not after
heuristics.condition: When an F-1 student has violated their status (e.g., dropped below full-time without DSO approval)
heuristics.action: Initiate USCIS reinstatement application immediately and no later than the 5-month mark from the date of the violation
heuristics.because: After 5 months out of status, reinstatement requires proving exceptional circumstances — a significantly higher and harder-to-meet standard that USCIS rarely grants

heuristics.rule: Count only ONE online course (up to 3 credits) toward your full-time enrollment minimum per semester
heuristics.condition: When an F-1 student is registering for courses that include online or distance-learning sections
heuristics.action: Ensure remaining credits needed to hit the full-time minimum (12 for undergrad, 9–12 for master's, 7+ for doctoral) come from in-person courses
heuristics.because: Federal regulations cap the online course credit that counts toward F-1 full-time load at one 3-credit course per semester — additional online courses do not satisfy the enrollment requirement

heuristics.rule: Do not treat summer non-enrollment logic as transferable to fall or spring semesters
heuristics.condition: When an F-1 student considers skipping enrollment in a fall or spring semester without authorization
heuristics.action: Enroll full-time in all fall and spring semesters unless one of the 4 RCL exceptions applies and has been approved by the DSO
heuristics.because: The annual vacation exception only applies to summer; applying it to fall or spring results in immediate out-of-status

heuristics.rule: Use the 60-day grace period deliberately — do not let it expire passively
heuristics.condition: When an F-1 student completes their academic program
heuristics.action: Within 60 days of program completion, take a concrete action: apply for OPT, transfer to another school, change immigration status, or depart the US
heuristics.because: The grace period is not an extension of status — it is a window to take action; failing to act within it results in unlawful presence accrual

decision_factors.factor: Whether DSO authorization was obtained before or after a course drop
decision_factors.why_it_matters: This single sequencing fact determines whether the student is in status or out of status — the action itself (dropping below full-time) is not the violation; doing it without prior DSO approval is
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Check whether the DSO has entered the RCL authorization in SEVIS and the student has received written confirmation before any course drop is submitted to the registrar

decision_factors.factor: Which of the 4 RCL exceptions applies to the student's situation
decision_factors.why_it_matters: DSOs have zero discretion to create exceptions outside the 4 federally defined categories — if the student's situation does not fit one of the 4, RCL authorization is legally unavailable regardless of how sympathetic the circumstances are
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Match the student's situation to: (1) academic difficulty, (2) medical condition with licensed provider documentation, (3) final semester with fewer credits needed, or (4) improper course level placement

decision_factors.factor: Time elapsed since the student fell out of status
decision_factors.why_it_matters: The 5-month threshold is a legal dividing line — under 5 months allows standard reinstatement; over 5 months requires proving exceptional circumstances, which USCIS rarely approves
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Calculate the exact date the violation began (date course was dropped without authorization or date enrollment fell below minimum) and count calendar days from that date

decision_factors.factor: History of repeated or willful F-1 violations
decision_factors.why_it_matters: USCIS denies reinstatement if the student has a record of repeated or willful status violations — a single accidental violation is treated very differently from a pattern
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Review the student's SEVIS record history with the DSO for any prior violations, previous reinstatement applications, or prior unauthorized enrollment drops

decision_factors.factor: Type of academic program and its specific full-time credit hour threshold
decision_factors.why_it_matters: The full-time minimum varies by program level — an incorrect assumption about the required credit load can lead to unknowing violations
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Confirm with DSO: Undergrad = 12+ credits; Master's = 9–12 (school-dependent); Doctoral = 7+ credits; English Language Program = 18+ hours/week

decision_factors.factor: Whether the student is in their final semester
decision_factors.why_it_matters: Final semester students are the only group that can carry fewer credits as a matter of course completion need — this exception is commonly underused by students who don't realize it applies to them
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Confirm with DSO that the reduced credits are literally all that remains to complete degree requirements — not just a preference for lighter load in the last term

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Taking multiple online courses to reduce commute or manage workload
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Only one online course counts toward the full-time enrollment minimum — a student taking 12 credits all online is technically out of status even though they are enrolled full-time by credit count
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Students in online-heavy programs or those who shift to online courses mid-semester for convenience without consulting their DSO

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Waiting to see if the situation resolves before contacting the DSO about a potential enrollment problem
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Every day of waiting without DSO authorization after dropping below full-time is a day of accrued out-of-status time, counting toward the 5-month reinstatement window — delay shrinks the window for easier recovery
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Students dealing with medical crises, mental health episodes, or family emergencies who delay administrative action while managing the personal situation

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Relying on the school to manage and monitor your F-1 compliance
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: F-1 status maintenance is legally the student's responsibility — schools report violations in SEVIS but are not obligated to proactively prevent individual students from falling out of status
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Students who come from educational systems with strong institutional oversight and assume the university will alert them before a violation becomes serious

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Applying for reinstatement after 5 months out of status to avoid the cost and complexity of reapplying for a visa abroad
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Post-5-month reinstatement requires demonstrating exceptional circumstances — a standard most students cannot meet — making the gamble likely to fail and leaving the student with fewer options than if they had left and re-entered
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Students who delay seeking help hoping the problem will resolve itself or who are unaware of the 5-month rule

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Enrolling concurrently at two SEVIS-approved schools to satisfy requirements
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Only the primary (degree-granting) school handles SEVIS reporting — if the student and secondary school do not coordinate, credits from the secondary school may not be recognized for full-time enrollment purposes in SEVIS
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Students taking courses at community colleges or other institutions to supplement their primary program

failure_patterns.pattern: Dropping below full-time enrollment and assuming the DSO can fix it retroactively
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students conflate the DSO's administrative authority with legal authority — DSOs can update SEVIS records but cannot retroactively authorize an RCL that was never approved before the drop occurred
failure_patterns.consequence: Student is immediately out of status from the date of the unauthorized drop; must file USCIS reinstatement application; may be barred from re-entry if they travel internationally
warning_signs: Student contacts DSO after already submitting course withdrawal
Student asks 'can you backdate the approval?' or 'can we say I told you earlier?'
Student was unaware they needed DSO approval before dropping

failure_patterns.pattern: Medical emergency causes enrollment drop without DSO involvement, and student waits until health improves to seek help
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students in crisis prioritize health over administrative process — understandably — but are unaware that the clock for status violation and the 5-month reinstatement window starts immediately
failure_patterns.consequence: By the time the student seeks DSO help, weeks or months of out-of-status time have accrued, potentially pushing them past the 5-month window for standard reinstatement
warning_signs: Student mentions a health crisis that coincided with a semester where they dropped or stopped attending courses
Student is unsure exactly when they fell below full-time enrollment
Student has not spoken to DSO since the health event began

failure_patterns.pattern: Treating summer break non-enrollment as a model for other semester breaks
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: The summer vacation rule is a genuine exception that students overgeneralize — they reason that if they can skip summer, they can take a semester off in fall or spring with the same logic
failure_patterns.consequence: Student falls out of status during fall or spring non-enrollment; violation is not discovered until the next SEVIS check or until they apply for OPT/H-1B sponsorship
warning_signs: Student asks 'Can I take a semester off like I did over summer?'
Student is planning a gap semester in fall or spring for personal or financial reasons without DSO consultation

failure_patterns.pattern: Counting all enrolled credits — including multiple online courses — as satisfying the full-time requirement
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students see total credit count on their transcript and assume it satisfies F-1 requirements without understanding the in-person credit rule for online courses
failure_patterns.consequence: Student is technically out of status despite being 'enrolled full-time' by credit count — violation may not surface until OPT or H-1B application when SEVIS history is reviewed
warning_signs: Student's schedule shows more than one online or distance-learning course
Student enrolled primarily online due to work schedule, convenience, or COVID-era habits that continued
Student has never had the online course rule explained by their DSO

failure_patterns.pattern: Allowing the 60-day grace period to expire without taking any qualifying action
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students believe the grace period extends their authorized stay rather than understanding it is only a window to take action — they treat it as a 60-day vacation
failure_patterns.consequence: Student begins accruing unlawful presence after the grace period ends; this can trigger 3-year or 10-year bars to re-entry if they subsequently depart the US
warning_signs: Student graduated more than 30 days ago and has not applied for OPT, transferred schools, or changed status
Student is 'figuring out next steps' without urgency after program completion
Student is unaware that the grace period has a hard end date

diagnostic_questions.question: Did you obtain written DSO authorization in SEVIS before dropping below full-time enrollment — or did you drop first and contact the DSO after?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the student is currently in status or already out of status — this single sequencing question determines the entire remediation path
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Which of the 4 RCL exceptions was cited, and is the supporting documentation (e.g., medical records) on file with the DSO?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: How many days ago did the drop occur? You may be in a reinstatement scenario — calculate time elapsed against the 5-month window immediately

diagnostic_questions.question: How long ago did the status violation begin — and has it been more than 5 months?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the student qualifies for standard USCIS reinstatement or must meet the much harder 'exceptional circumstances' standard
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Document any exceptional circumstances carefully; consult an immigration attorney immediately — standard reinstatement is likely unavailable
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: File reinstatement application as soon as possible; every additional day of delay reduces the window further

diagnostic_questions.question: Does your situation fit one of the 4 federally defined RCL exceptions: academic difficulty, medical condition, final semester, or improper course level placement?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether DSO authorization for reduced course load is even legally available — if the answer is no, the student cannot legally drop below full-time regardless of the hardship
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Do you have the required documentation ready (e.g., licensed physician letter for medical RCL) to present to the DSO before submitting the course drop?
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: The student must maintain full-time enrollment or explore other options (medical leave of absence, program withdrawal, status change) — RCL is not available

diagnostic_questions.question: How many of your currently enrolled courses are online or distance-learning, and are you counting all of them toward your full-time minimum?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the student has an undiscovered enrollment compliance problem — students who assume all credits count equally may be unknowingly out of status
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Only one online course (up to 3 credits) counts toward the F-1 full-time minimum — recalculate in-person credits and assess whether the minimum is actually met
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Confirm the single online course being counted does not exceed 3 credits and that remaining credits are from in-person sections

diagnostic_questions.question: Have you had any prior F-1 status violations, SEVIS terminations, or reinstatement applications in your history?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether a new reinstatement application faces a higher bar — USCIS denies reinstatement for students with a record of repeated or willful violations
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Consult an immigration attorney before filing — the prior history must be addressed and the current violation must be clearly distinguishable as non-willful
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Proceed with standard reinstatement process if within the 5-month window; document that the current violation was inadvertent

diagnostic_questions.question: Is this your final semester, and are the credits you are enrolled in literally all that remains to complete your degree?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Whether the student qualifies for the final semester RCL exception — this is an underused pathway that allows below-full-time enrollment without the other exception criteria
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Confirm this with your DSO and have it authorized in SEVIS — you do not need medical documentation; the degree audit confirming remaining requirements is sufficient
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: The final semester exception does not apply; assess which of the other 3 RCL exceptions, if any, fits the situation

industry_realities.common_belief: The school (DSO) is responsible for monitoring and maintaining your F-1 status — they will catch problems and alert you
industry_realities.actual_reality: F-1 status maintenance is entirely the student's legal responsibility; DSOs report changes to SEVIS but are not obligated to proactively prevent individual students from falling out of compliance
industry_realities.implication: Students must self-monitor their enrollment status every semester and proactively contact the DSO before any change that could affect full-time status — waiting to be told is a losing strategy

industry_realities.common_belief: Dropping one or two courses is a minor administrative matter that can be undone or explained away
industry_realities.actual_reality: F-1 status operates as a binary — you are either in status or out of status; dropping one course that takes you below the full-time minimum without DSO approval is a federal immigration violation with no automatic remedy
industry_realities.implication: Students must treat any enrollment change that could affect full-time status as an immigration event requiring DSO consultation, not just an academic scheduling decision

industry_realities.common_belief: A compassionate DSO can approve a Reduced Course Load retroactively for a student who had a genuine emergency
industry_realities.actual_reality: DSOs have zero legal authority to authorize RCL retroactively — federal regulations require authorization before the course drop; DSOs who attempt retroactive authorization are themselves violating SEVIS reporting obligations
industry_realities.implication: Students in emerging crises (medical, family, mental health) must contact the DSO while the crisis is active — even a brief call or email before dropping courses protects status in a way no retroactive action can

industry_realities.common_belief: If you've been out of status for a while, it's better to quietly re-enroll full-time and hope no one notices before applying for OPT or other benefits
industry_realities.actual_reality: SEVIS contains a complete enrollment history; any gap in full-time enrollment without authorized RCL will surface during OPT application review, H-1B sponsorship background checks, or visa renewal interviews — and misrepresentation of the gap is a separate, more serious violation
industry_realities.implication: Students who discover a past status violation must address it proactively through reinstatement or legal counsel rather than hoping it goes undetected — the cost of discovery later vastly exceeds the cost of remediation now

industry_realities.common_belief: Summer break means you can freely skip enrollment without any immigration consequence
industry_realities.actual_reality: Summer non-enrollment is permitted under the annual vacation rule only — it does not extend to any other term, and students must plan to enroll full-time in the fall following summer break
industry_realities.implication: Students must confirm with their DSO that their planned summer non-enrollment qualifies under the annual vacation rule (not all students in all program stages qualify automatically) and must not carry this assumption into fall or spring planning

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Drops courses first to resolve the immediate academic or personal pressure, then contacts DSO afterward to 'get it sorted out' — assumes the fix is administrative
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Contacts DSO before any course drop, describes the situation, obtains written authorization in SEVIS, and only then submits the course drop to the registrar
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: The sequence is the entire legal difference between staying in status and being out of status — informed students know this; uninformed students learn it the hard way after the violation has already occurred

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: Understanding of who owns F-1 compliance
junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Assumes the international student office will send reminders, catch issues, and flag when something is wrong — treats compliance as the school's job
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Treats F-1 status as personal legal liability — proactively tracks their own enrollment every semester, reads DSO communications, and consults before any change rather than waiting to be told
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Students who outsource compliance monitoring to the school are statistically the ones who discover violations too late to use standard remediation pathways

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: Knowledge of the RCL exception framework
junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Believes that genuine hardship — financial stress, mental health struggles, family emergencies — is a sufficient basis for dropping below full-time, and that the DSO will find a way to make it work
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Knows that only 4 federally defined exceptions exist, that DSOs cannot create new ones regardless of circumstances, and maps their situation to one of the 4 before approaching the DSO
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Students who expect hardship-based flexibility may be misled by well-meaning DSOs who 'do their best' — understanding the hard limits prevents reliance on advice that cannot legally be acted upon

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Discovers they fell out of status and waits to address it until life stabilizes, a new semester starts, or an OPT application triggers a review — underestimates the time-sensitivity
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Upon discovering any potential status violation, immediately calculates days elapsed against the 5-month reinstatement window, contacts DSO and immigration attorney same day, and treats it as an urgent legal matter
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: The 5-month reinstatement window closes silently — students who delay because the problem feels distant often find themselves past the standard threshold with dramatically fewer options

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Registers for whichever mix of online and in-person courses fits their schedule, counts total credits as satisfying the full-time requirement, and never flags the course format breakdown to the DSO
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Explicitly tracks how many of their enrolled credits come from online sections, ensures only one online course is counted toward the full-time minimum, and confirms the remaining credits are from in-person sections each semester
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: An online-heavy semester can produce a hidden status violation that remains undetected for years and then surfaces catastrophically during OPT or H-1B processing when SEVIS history is reviewed
